coredata.dc:title: Ratiometric fluorescent sensing of melatonin based on inner filter effect and smartphone established detection
coredata.prism:publicationName: Spectrochimica Acta Part A: Molecular and Biomolecular Spectroscopy
coredata.dc:creator: Kumar, Himanshu
Obrai, Sangeeta
coredata.dc:description: Melatonin (MLT) is a crucial neurohormone having inhibitory effects over various types of cancer. In this work, 3,6-Diaminocarbazole (DAC), a fluorescent probe is utilized to detect MLT in a highly sensitive, selective and facile way. The unique feature of present work is that MLT is sensed by ratiometric fluorescent technique based on the inner filter effect (IFE) using DAC at an emission wavelength of 310 nm. As a result, a noticeable change in color from red to cyan is observed and the quantitative analysis of fluorescence signals at these wavelengths are used to detect MLT observing a linear relationship between the ratio of emission intensities and the concentration of MLT over a linear range of 0 to 78 μM. DAC can accurately measure the detailed quantity of MLT with a limit of detection of 30 nM and has proved to be an efficient sensing probe due to its excellent molar absorptivity and high photoluminescence quantum yield (PLQY). Sensing characterization was carried out UV–Vis, steady-state, and time- resolved fluorescence spectroscopic techniques. The smartphone app “RGB colour detector” value has been successfully linked with the considerable detectable color changes of DAC on addition of MLT. HOMO-LUMO have been calculated using DFT with B3LYP/6-31G(d,p) level and band gaps of 3.77 eV and 4.91 eV were found for DAC and MLT, respectively. Electrons are not allowed energetically to transfer from MLT to DAC, as is evident from their band gaps. Therefore, IFE can be considered the foremost method in fluorescence quenching of present investigation. The developed sensor was verified by spiking of MLT in human serum.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Inner filter effect (IFE)
3,6-Diaminocarbazole (DAC)
Melatonin (MLT)
Ratiometric Fluorescence (RF)
Limit of Detection (LOD)
Fluorescence spectroscopy
Density Functional Theory (DFT).

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Detection of MLT in human blood samples

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: Authors are highly thankful to Ministry of Human Resource and Development (MHRD), New Delhi for providing research assistantship to Mr. Himanshu Kumar, one of the authors and Dr. B.R. Ambedkar National Institute of Technology, Jalandhar for providing research infrastructure.

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: (a) UV–Vis absorption of MLT; fluorescence excitation and fluorescence emission band of 3,6-diaminocarbazole (DAC). (b) Fluorescence spectra of the 3,6-diaminocarbazole (DAC) at different excitation wavelengths.

(a) Fluorescence spectra of the DAC recorded with increasing concentration (from top to bottom, 0 to 78 μM) under 448 nm excitation. (b) The relation between fluorescence intensity and amounts of MLT. (c) Steady-state absorbance spectra of DAC with successive addition of MLT. (d) Fluorescence decay curve of the DAC with the addition of various concentrations of MLT. (e) CIE chromaticity diagram for MLT analysis.
(a) Fluorescence spectra of DAC at room temperature with time(sec) (b) Time-dependent fluorescence response of DAC (c) Fluorescence intensity of DAC with aq sodium chloride solution.
Histogram of fluorescence quenching efficiency of DAC towards MLT and other analogous analytes.
Frontier Molecular Orbitals (HOMO-LUMO) of DAC and MLT.
Smartphone-based determination of MLT ions by DAC in ethanol-H2O.
Comparative performance previously reported melatonin sensor.

row.entry: Types of sensors for Melatonin reported
Protein nano Dots conjugated AuNP, poly-Lysine biointerface
Highly Sensitive and Selective Ratiometric Fluorescence Detection of Melatonin Using 3,6-Diaminocarbazole

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Detection of MLT in human blood samples.

head.ce:title: Ratiometric fluorescent sensing of melatonin based on inner filter effect and smartphone established detection

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Chemistry, Dr. B.R. Ambedkar National Institute of Technology, Jalandhar, Punjab 144011, India

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Dr. B.R. Ambedkar National Institute of Technology

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Department of Chemistry, Dr. B.R. Ambedkar National Institute of Technology, Jalandhar, Punjab 144011, India

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Melatonin (MLT) is a crucial neurohormone having inhibitory effects over various types of cancer. In this work, 3,6-Diaminocarbazole (DAC), a fluorescent probe is utilized to detect MLT in a highly sensitive, selective and facile way. The unique feature of present work is that MLT is sensed by ratiometric fluorescent technique based on the inner filter effect (IFE) using DAC at an emission wavelength of 310 nm. As a result, a noticeable change in color from red to cyan is observed and the quantitative analysis of fluorescence signals at these wavelengths are used to detect MLT observing a linear relationship between the ratio of emission intensities and the concentration of MLT over a linear range of 0 to 78 μM. DAC can accurately measure the detailed quantity of MLT with a limit of detection of 30 nM and has proved to be an efficient sensing probe due to its excellent molar absorptivity and high photoluminescence quantum yield (PLQY). Sensing characterization was carried out UV–Vis, steady-state, and time- resolved fluorescence spectroscopic techniques. The smartphone app “RGB colour detector” value has been successfully linked with the considerable detectable color changes of DAC on addition of MLT. HOMO-LUMO have been calculated using DFT with B3LYP/6-31G(d,p) level and band gaps of 3.77 eV and 4.91 eV were found for DAC and MLT, respectively. Electrons are not allowed energetically to transfer from MLT to DAC, as is evident from their band gaps. Therefore, IFE can be considered the foremost method in fluorescence quenching of present investigation. The developed sensor was verified by spiking of MLT in human serum.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: The authors do not have permission to share data.

ce:section.ce:para: Melatonin, a neuroharmone, is mainly secreted by the pineal gland and is synthesized from its precursor tryptophan [1]. Melatonin plays important biological role in regulating the circadian rhythm [2], sleep [3], abnormal growth of cells leading to cancer [4], metabolic effects [5], anti-inflammatory functions [6] and antioxidant effects [7,8]. The irregular secretion of melatonin may lead to autism spectrum disorder (ASD) [9–12]. Besides it, melatonin plays a crucial role in foetus development [13]. MLT had been detected using a variety of approaches, including high-performance liquid chromatography [14–17], electro-chemical [18–25], mass spectrometry [26–31], fluorimetry [32–36] and voltametry [37–44] in the previously reported investigations. Successful real sample analyses [33,45,46] were also performed using voltammetry, colorimetry and fluorimetry. The latter technique is not much explored for melatonin sensing but now it is gaining a lot of attention and can prove a better alternative than those mentioned above due to its outstanding features such as simplicity, high sensitivity, low cost, practicability and good selectivity. Moreover, till now, the fluorescence-based probes for MLT are mainly single wavelength intensity modulation type sensors . One limitation is that single emission sensors based on quenching, rather than fluorescence color change, tend to be less sensitive and vulnerable to fluctuations in the excitation light source. These limitations can be overcome by using a recently emerging technique of radiometric fluorescence probes for analyte detection [47]. Two or more fluorescence signals are observed by varying the wavelengths in ratiometric fluorescence. The calculated intensity ratio of observed signals contributes to lessen the interference of environmental effects and enhancing the sensitivity of quantitative measurements [48]. High fluorescence quantum yield and molar absorption coefficient are the prerequisites of the fluorophore at the excitation wavelength. Ratiometric sensing along with enormous applications has been reported earlier. Among them, some of these are with organic dyes [49,50], metal organic frameworks (MOFs) [51,52], nanocomplexes [53], gold and silver nanoclusters [54,55], carbon dots [56], quantum dots [57], hybrid carbon and quantum dots [58], Si nanoparticles [59,60], up conversion nanoparticles [61,62]. Inner filter effect (IFE) is one of the phenomena of fluorescence quenching that takes place as a result of the absorption of either both excitation and emission light or one of these. Moreover, the life-time of the donor will remain constant [62]. This characteristic has inspired the creation of various innovative fluorescence assays. The IFE-based strategy does not call for covalent tying of a receptor to a fluorophore or surface modifying of fluorescent materials. For example, carbon dots have been used as a fluorescent nanoprobe to detect acetone via IFE [63]. Organophosphorus pesticides were sensed and evaluated with IFE between Au nanoparticles and quantum dots [64]. Literature survey has revealed that carbazole based sensors have been used for the detection of toxic ions like Fe3+ [65], Cu2+ [66], Hg2+ [67], Al3+ [68], CN– [69] ions and nitroaromatics (explosives) in a highly sensitive manner [70–72]. Inspired by the above-mentioned works, we attempt to demonstrate new IFE-based ratiometric fluorescence sensors using DAC as it is highly biocompatible with very low toxicity.

Herein, we reported the ratiometric fluorescent sensor based on IFE (inner filter effect) for the detection of MLT using 3,6-Diaminocarbazole (DAC) as probe for the first time. The IFE only occurs effectively when the absorption band of the absorber overlaps well with the fluorophore. DAC displayed an emission peak at 448 nm upon excitation at 310 nm. The photoluminescence of DAC showed a sharp quenching response at 448 nm upon the addition of MLT. On the other side, the reaction of DAC with MLT generated a new emission at 343 nm that was enhanced with the increasing concentration of MLT. The two signal changes were utilized for the ratiometric fluorescence method to detect MLT. This sensor shows the following benefits: first, because it is an RF probe designed for MLT, general effects may be minimized, resulting in more accurate detection. Second, it has a very low detection limit of 30 nM towards MLT and is highly sensitive, selective, facile, cost-effective and reliable approach. A thorough investigation of UV–Vis, steady state and time-resolved fluorescence spectroscopy was carried out to explore the hypothesized sensing mechanism. “RGB color detector”, the smart phone app. has also been proved very helpful in detecting the color changes for sensing purposes and assessing LOD.

3,6-Diaminocarbazole (9H-carbazole-3,6-diamine;>98.0%) was purchased from TCI chemical melatonin (minimum;98.0%) was purchased from Sigma Aldrich. Deionized water was used for all experiments, dilution and sample preparation.

The fluorescence emission and UV–Vis absorbance measurements were recorded on SHIMADZU RF-6000 Spectro fluorescence and AGILENT respectively, Time-resolved fluorescence study was conducted single photon counting (TCSPC) (Horiba Jobin Yvon, Fluorocube with TBX-04D photomultiplier).

The stock solutions of DAC and MLT were prepared in a mixture of ethanol and DI water (50: 50 ratio). MLT sensing was conducted in the aqueous phase by adding its solution of varied concentrations to 0––78 μM probe solution of DAC. The fluorescence was measured at 310 nm for excitation and 445 nm for emission.

These were conducted in order to better understand the sensing mechanism of MLT. Fig. 1 a depicts high spectral overlap of the absorption and excitation band of MLT and DAC respectively whereas the emission and excitation band of the latter have relatively lesser overlap foreseeing the inner filter effect [73]. DAC showed the maximum emission intensity near 448 nm upon excitation at 310 nm (Fig. 1b) and on addition of MLT, the intensity of emission peak gradually decreased and on addition of MLT, the intensity of emission peak gradually decreased. In contrast, a new emission peak appeared at 343 nm that evidently increased with a well-defined isosbestic point at 405 nm, as shown in Fig. 2 a. A linear relationship between the ratio of the emission intensities and concentration of MLT in the range of 0 to 78 μM was found indicating that DAC can accurately measure the detailed quantity of MLT with detection limit as low as 30 nM (Fig. 2b). A comparative study of previously reported MLT sensors with the biosensor (DAC) in the present work demonstrated improved performance for MLT detection (Table 1 ). CIE 1931 chromaticity was illustrated (Fig. 2e) for MLT titration based on the fluorescence spectrum data depicting the color change from red to green area with the addition of MLT and was consistent with the change of fluorescence visual color.

Some investigations are conducted to learn more about the sensing process. The excitation and absorption spectrum of DAC and MLT respectively exhibited a significant overlap, suggesting that MLT can quench the fluorescence of DAC via IFE (Fig. 1a). Also, the fluorescence decay curves of DAC are exhibiting a high degree of consistency in the absence and presence of MLT (Fig. 2d). IFE appears to be the most probable mechanism for the excellent sensitivity and selectivity of DAC as the average fluorescence lifetime of DAC (4.76 ns) remains unchanged with/without the addition of MLT. This excludes the possibility of FRET as the energy transfer leads to decrease in lifetime of donor unlike PET and IFE [48]. In the former phenomenon, the lifetime of probe is extended whereas in the latter no change is observed. Moreover, the absorption spectra of melatonin does not overlap with the emission spectra of DAC and this also excludes the possibility of FRET and confirmed that the IFE mechanism is responsible for melatonin sensing (Fig. 1(a)). The UV–visible spectra of DAC with MLT also showed a little increase in the absorption strength without changing the wavelength of DAC (Fig. 2c). These rule out the idea of aggregating DAC chains and creating any ground-state charge transfer complex between MLT and DAC. Even electron transport pathways may be connected to the fluorescence quenching. Theoretical investigation of sensing mechanism is also performed to elucidate the sensing mechanism using DFT by calculating the energy of frontier molecular orbitals.

The DFT (Density functional theory) was used for theoretical study of the interactions of MLT with DAC using basis set 631G (d,p) of GAUSSIAN 16 [74]. The frontier molecular orbital energies of each of the two species have been calculated and empirical formula is then used to obtain the HOMO and LUMO [75,76]. The B3LYP technique in mode enabled the EHOMO and ELUMO of DAC to be determined as −4.21 eV and −0.44 eV respectively while the same for MLT are −5.19 and −0.28 eV (Fig. 5 a). It is clear from (Fig. 5b), that photo-excited electrons in MLT are not energetically permitted to undergo electron transfer from the DAC to the MLT. Hence, the IFE may be viewed as a significant fluorescence process in this work.

Recent days have seen a lot of interest in the development of smartphone applications for sensors. The smartphone-based sensors detection device is affordable, user-friendly, safe, and straightforward [77–82]. In order to correlate the fluctuations in RGB values with the colour changes of the probe from yellowish orange to wine red following addition of MLT, the complex was integrated with a smartphone. The three colours Red, Green, and Blue are represented by whole numbers on the traditional RGB scale, with values ranging from 0 to 240 for each channel. The RGB values correspond to the colours black and white are 0 and 240, respectively. Based on this concept, a free mobile app (RGB Colour Value) was downloaded on a Realme 6i. The RGB values of the probe with varying MLT concentrations were observed for changes in colour. Using the mobile rear camera, RGB values of DAC in the absence and presence of increasing quantities of MLT were recorded. The calculated R/G was plotted vs the amount of MLT, and the resulting graph is depicted in Fig. 6 with an assessed LOD of 1.46 µM, and a linear plot of the data shows that the correlation coefficient is R2 = 0.9973. These results demonstrated that the low-cost current smartphone-based technology can be utilised to measure MLT ions on-site [83].

ce:section.ce:section-title: Detection of MLT in human blood samples
ce:section.ce:para: An effective analytical strategy for accurate measurement of MLT levels in human blood has been developed. Reaping the advantages of high sensitivity and strong selectivity, DAC as biosensor was initially employed to detect MLT in human blood serum with pH = 7. The results obtained have been presented in Table 2 . The accuracy of the sample analysis was assessed by introducing a precise volume of standard MLT into clinical serum samples and thereby computing its recovery. The prospective of biosensor for specifically tracing MLT levels in human serum was examined in light of the foregoing findings. The human blood serum was centrifuged many times for a period of 15 min with 5000 rpm. The supernatant fluid was 1000 times diluted before being spiked with varying MLT concentrations (0, 5, 10, 15,20,25, and 30 μM). The range of sample recoveries employing this detection strategy lies between 98.8 and 104.2% as tabulated in Table 2. The efficiency of the planned strategy is demonstrated by the desired recoveries for recognizing its practical applications [86].

The ratiometric fluorescence method based on IFE (inner filter effect) is developed first time for highly sensitive and selective detection of MLT employing 3,6-diaminocarbazole (DAC) as probes and established a very low LOD (30 nM). Also, the smartphone-based detection of MLT is carried out with an assessed LOD of 1.46 µM. The fluorescence quenching efficiency of DAC with MLT is quite high relative to other analogous analytes. The absorption and excitation band of MLT and DAC respectively have large spectral overlap while the excitation and emission spectral overlap is fairly good. Moreover, the lifespan of DAC remains intact in the presence or absence of MLT attributing fluorescence quenching to IFE. The sensing applications of DAC in high-salt environments, exhibited strong stability against changes in ionic strength in aqueous solutions. DAC showed high photostability also on its exposure to UV–Vis light. Also, the electrons are also not allowed energetically to get transferred from MLT to DAC as is obvious from their band gaps. Therefore, UV–Vis, fluorescence, time- resolved fluorescence spectroscopic and HOMO-LUMO studies emphasize that the ratiometric fluorescence based on IFE is the prime technique in the aforementioned work.

Keeping in view of the high sensitivity, selectivity and reliability along with the affluence and cost effectiveness, the developed assay can prove a very promising intrant for MLT screening in biomedical research. In nutshell, this IFE and smartphone-based investigation will open up a fresh avenue for determining MLT in the real world.
Himanshu Kumar: Conceptualization, Resources, Investigation, Writing – review & editing, Data curation, Writing – original draft, Methodology. Sangeeta Obrai: Supervision.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: Authors are highly thankful to Ministry of Human Resource and Development (MHRD), New Delhi for providing research assistantship to Mr. Himanshu Kumar, one of the authors and Dr. B.R. Ambedkar National Institute of Technology, Jalandhar for providing research infrastructure.
